title: Secrets Hidden in Code
intro: A junior developer just pushed their first feature. It works great, but there's a critical security problem hiding in their code -- it's like leaving a spare key under the doormat for anyone to find. API keys should NEVER be committed to source code; they belong in environment variables. Find the line that exposes a secret.

teach.title: Secrets in Code Are Like Passwords on Sticky Notes
teach.body: Imagine writing your bank password on a sticky note and leaving it on your desk at a coffee shop. Putting an API key directly in your code is the same thing -- anyone who can see your code (teammates, GitHub users, hackers) can see your secret and use it.

teach.title: Hardcoded Keys Are Like Spare Keys Under the Doormat
teach.body: Everyone knows to check under the doormat for a spare house key -- it's the first place a burglar looks. Hardcoded API keys in source code are the same: bots scan every public GitHub repo looking for patterns like 'sk_live_' or 'AKIA'. It's not a matter of if someone finds your key, but when. Use a secure lockbox (environment variables) instead of the doormat.

teach.title: Why Hardcoded Secrets Are Dangerous
teach.body:
When you put a secret directly in source code:

1. It gets saved in **Git history forever** -- even if you delete it later
2. Anyone with access to the repo can **find and use it**
3. If the repo goes public, **bots scan GitHub constantly** for leaked keys

Real-world impact: leaked API keys have led to stolen data and surprise bills of thousands of dollars.

teach.title: The Right Way: Environment Variables
teach.annotation: Environment variables live outside your code, so they never get committed to Git. Store them in a .env file locally and in your hosting provider's settings for production.

teach.body: A leaked Stripe key can rack up thousands of dollars in fraudulent charges on YOUR account before you even notice. When you're vibecoding your next SaaS product, keeping secrets out of code isn't just best practice -- it's protecting your bottom line and your clients' payment data.
Never put API keys, passwords, or tokens directly in your code. Use environment variables instead, and add .env to your .gitignore so it's never committed.

exercise.prompt: This code handles real payments with Stripe. Find the line that creates a serious security vulnerability.
exercise.explanation: A live Stripe secret key is hardcoded directly in the source file. If this gets pushed to GitHub (even a private repo that later becomes public, or gets forked), anyone who finds it can charge credit cards using your account. The fix is to use environment variables via process.env. Options B, C, and D are all still ways of storing secrets in files that could be committed -- only environment variables keep secrets completely out of the codebase.
